topic: reflecting on season — good year for shrimp prices

turns.content: today is good day friend. sit here drinking coffee, thinking about this season. price very strong. maybe best in 3 year, you know. I feel grateful
That's a great feeling — sitting back with a coffee after a strong season. What's driving the good prices this year?

export demand go up. some country have problem with their shrimp — disease, bad weather. so they buy more from vietnam. and here in mekong, many farmer lose stock earlier this year from disease. less shrimp on market, buyer need more. supply down, demand up. simple math, you know

Simple math, and it worked in your favor because your stock stayed healthy while others lost theirs. That's not luck — that's the 4am mornings and the careful management paying off. How much better are prices compared to last season?

maybe 15-20% better than last year. I hear some farmer getting around 180,000 dong per kilo for good size. last year same shrimp maybe 150,000-155,000, you know. big difference when you multiply by whole harvest

That 25,000-30,000 dong per kilo difference adds up fast. On a decent harvest, that's millions of dong extra just from market timing. And the best part is you didn't have to do anything different — just keep your shrimp healthy and the market came to you.

yes. buyer come to me this season. I not have to go find buyer, you know. they call, they come to pond. that is nice feeling. but I not greedy, friend. I sell when shrimp ready, not hold in pond hoping price go even higher

That's the smart play. Holding shrimp in the pond waiting for higher prices is a gamble — every extra day means more feed cost, more disease risk, and they can oversize past the premium range. Have you seen farmers get burned by waiting too long?

oh yes. my neighbor down the road, last year he hold 2 extra week because price was climbing. then disease hit his pond. he lose maybe 40% stock, you know. what he save in higher price he lose ten time in dead shrimp. very expensive lesson. I tell him — pond is not bank. you cannot keep money in pond, it can die

Pond is not a bank — that's perfect. Money in a bank sits safe. Shrimp in a pond eat feed, produce waste, and can die overnight. Sell when they're ready, lock in the profit, move to the next cycle. Your neighbor learned the hard way what you already knew from experience.

yes. so this season, good price, I sell on time, shrimp healthy. now what I do with extra money? little bit fix thing on farm that need repair. save the rest. good year you must save because next year maybe not so good, you know. that is farming life

Fix and save — the most disciplined approach. A lot of people would celebrate and spend during a good year, but you know that farming runs in cycles. Up and down. Using the good year to repair infrastructure and build a buffer for the lean years is what separates farmers who last decades from those who don't.

up and down like tide, friend. pond teach you patience. you stock the shrimp, you feed every day, you wait, you harvest. no shortcut. sometime tide high, sometime low. but if you patient and careful, more high than low over the year. 40 year I learn this

Forty years of tides going in and out, and you're still here — that says everything. The farmers who chase shortcuts or panic during low tides are the ones who quit. The ones who stay steady through both, like you, end up with a lifetime of good seasons outweighing the bad.

Healthy shrimp, strong prices, family doing okay — sounds like a season worth being grateful for.

yes. healthy shrimp, good price, family okay. what more can old farmer ask for, you know. sometime life hard but day like today I sit here, drink coffee, listen to bird, think about all the morning I walk pond in dark. it all add up. okay friend. I go check on pond now. enough philosophy for one morning. hah. thank you

Ha! Enough philosophy — back to the real work. You've earned that peaceful coffee and those good feelings. Enjoy the rest of the season, save smart, and keep those early mornings going. Take care, and here's to the next harvest being just as good.
